knowledges.content: Pisum sativum (garden pea) with controlled pollination. Seven traits studied: seed shape, seed colour, pod shape, pod colour, flower colour, flower position, stem height.
knowledges.label: pea_model

knowledges.content: Each individual carries exactly two copies of each heritable factor (one from each parent). Factors are discrete particles, not blends.
knowledges.label: two_allele_model

knowledges.content: F2 offspring of monohybrid crosses show dominant:recessive ratio $\approx 3{:}1$ across all seven traits studied by Mendel (1866).
knowledges.label: monohybrid_3_1

knowledges.content: F2 offspring of dihybrid crosses show phenotypic ratio $\approx 9{:}3{:}3{:}1$ for independent trait combinations.
knowledges.label: dihybrid_9_3_3_1

knowledges.content: F1 generation of crosses between true-breeding parents is phenotypically uniform, expressing only the dominant trait.
knowledges.label: f1_uniformity

knowledges.content: Blending inheritance predicts F2 offspring should show intermediate phenotypes with variance decreasing each generation — no discrete 3:1 ratio.
knowledges.label: blending_prediction

knowledges.content: Law of Segregation: the two alleles for a trait separate during gamete formation so each gamete carries exactly one allele.
knowledges.label: segregation_law

knowledges.content: Law of Independent Assortment: alleles of different genes assort independently during gamete formation.
knowledges.label: independent_assortment

knowledges.content: Hereditary information is encoded in discrete particulate factors (genes) that are transmitted unchanged between generations.
knowledges.label: particulate_inheritance

knowledges.content: Do genes on the same chromosome assort independently? Morgan's linkage experiments (1910) show exceptions.
knowledges.label: linkage_question

metadata.reason: Blending inheritance cannot produce discrete 3:1 ratios. The observed F2 ratio falsifies blending and supports particulate factors.
